# New approach to machine lifecycle support benefits your people, parts, and processes

August 3, 2022

4 min read | [Nathan Owens III](https://blog.se.com/author/nathan-owens-iii/) & [Walter Koetter](https://blog.se.com/author/walter-koetter/)

Machine builders are looking for you to help them automate functions and make machines easier to use and maintain. They also are looking for more power while using less energy, modular designs to conserve space, and versatile components to increase flexibility, and reduce inventory.

Most importantly, they are looking to you for end-to-end solutions through a machine’s full lifecycle. That’s why the quality of your machine lifecycle support is increasingly critical. Machine lifecycle support has evolved to include training, managing inventory, and access to parts. This support benefits your customers in many ways — from assuring uptime and saving labor and maintenance costs, to increasing productivity and overall profitability.

Machine lifecycle support is a great opportunity to develop mutually beneficial relationships. You have opportunities for ongoing and additional revenue, and the customer has paths for realizing the full potential of their machine investments.

## Support model begins with pre-sale

An integrated machine lifecycle and operational support model is mostly split between your pre- and post-sales. The pre-sales is your project execution, from the concept/design phase through commissioning. Post-sales includes technical support and services.

Essential components of your machine lifecycle support should include:

## Technical support is about more than service agreements

As you build more automation and motion control into machines, and rely on more real-time data, your service agreements take on increasing importance from a technical support perspective. [Service agreements](https://www.se.com/us/en/work/services/field-services/industrial-automation/maintenance-and-support/automation-maintenance-support.jsp) should account for the scope of resources available for response and provide a process for technical support. A higher level of support might include remote monitoring, quicker response from field technicians, or access to research and development (R&D) via internal tech support for debugging.

Technical support should include:

## Services should cover end-to-end machine building needs

Like many machine builders, you are likely facing increasingly stringent demands from end users on productivity, safety, equipment availability, and performance optimization. Therefore, your optimal services need to cover end-to-end needs for machine builders and their end users, including:

## Grow your revenue opportunities by reselling support agreements

Your customers need to be able to respond to the end users as quickly as possible. The focus on machine support should be on the end users; they are the ones getting value from the investment in the machine.

The right machine building partner will help you grow revenue after the sale. There are now [suites of products in a single platform](https://www.se.com/us/en/work/campaign/innovation/industries.jsp) that are specifically designed to enable machine builders and manufacturers to monitor machines and sell after-market services and support to end users. These include:

## Transform your customer experience with the right machine lifecycle support partner

The right partner will not only provide you tangible benefits throughout the machine lifecycle, they will also provide the best possible customer experience and peace of mind. The right partner will be responsive to design, implementation, and service, but they also will be able to show you what is available as they develop technological advances. They will keep you engaged in the continuous process of research and development and how it impacts your machine lifecycles.

Learn how the right partner can help you improve your approach to machine lifecycle support.

# **The Importance of Managing Equipment Life Cycle**

In today’s competitive business landscape, effectively managing the life cycle of equipment is essential for maintaining a competitive edge. From an asset management and plant maintenance perspective, implementing robust strategies to manage equipment throughout its life cycle brings numerous benefits and advantages. By optimizing equipment performance, minimizing downtime, and extending operational lifespan, enterprises can maximize return on investment (ROI), reduce costs, and ensure smooth and efficient operations. Let’s explore why managing equipment life cycle is of paramount importance:

## **1. Maximizes the return on investment (ROI) for equipment**

Managing the equipment life cycle ensures that investments made in acquiring assets yield the highest possible returns. By implementing proactive maintenance strategies, equipment downtime and associated costs can be minimized, leading to increased productivity and revenue generation.

## **2. Extends the operational lifespan of equipment**

Proper maintenance and care throughout the life cycle of equipment significantly contribute to its longevity. Regular inspections, preventive maintenance, and timely repairs help identify and address potential issues before they escalate, thereby extending the operational lifespan of the equipment.

## **3. Reduces the risk of unexpected breakdowns and costly repairs**

Equipment breakdowns can lead to unplanned downtime, disrupt production schedules, and incur significant repair costs. By implementing a comprehensive equipment life cycle management approach, organizations can identify and mitigate potential risks, reducing the likelihood of unexpected breakdowns and minimizing associated expenses.

## **4. Optimizes equipment performance and efficiency**

Effective management of the equipment life cycle involves regular performance assessments and optimization initiatives. By monitoring equipment performance metrics, organizations can identify areas for improvement, implement necessary adjustments, and ensure equipment operates at peak efficiency levels.

## **5. Minimizes downtime and production disruptions**

Equipment failures and unplanned downtime can have severe repercussions on production schedules, customer satisfaction, and overall business operations. By adopting a proactive maintenance approach and implementing equipment life cycle management strategies, organizations can minimize downtime, enhance operational continuity, and meet customer expectations more effectively.

## **6. Improves safety by ensuring equipment is well-maintained and in good working condition**

Proper maintenance and regular inspections as part of equipment life cycle management contribute to a safer working environment. This is why condition monitoring is essential. By ensuring equipment is well-maintained and in good working condition through a robust condition monitoring system, the risk of accidents and injuries can be significantly reduced, promoting a safer workplace for employees.

## **7. Enhances productivity by minimizing equipment failures and associated downtime**

Unplanned equipment failures and downtime can lead to productivity losses and delays in production schedules. By managing the equipment life cycle effectively, organizations can minimize the occurrence of equipment failures, optimize maintenance schedules, and maximize productivity levels, leading to improved operational efficiency.

## **8. Reduces the need for emergency repairs and their associated costs**

Emergency repairs are often more expensive and time-consuming than planned maintenance activities. By implementing a structured equipment life cycle management approach, organizations can reduce the need for emergency repairs, resulting in cost savings and avoiding production disruptions caused by unexpected breakdowns.

## **9. Enables proactive maintenance planning and scheduling**

Managing the equipment life cycle allows organizations to plan and schedule maintenance activities in advance. By proactively identifying maintenance requirements based on equipment performance data and manufacturer recommendations, maintenance tasks can be planned and scheduled to minimize disruptions to operations and optimize resource allocation.

## **10. Helps identify and address potential reliability issues early on**

By closely monitoring equipment performance and analyzing maintenance data, organizations can identify early signs of potential reliability issues. This enables them to take timely corrective actions, such as conducting repairs, replacing faulty components, or adjusting maintenance procedures, before the issues escalate and cause significant operational problems.

## **11. Facilitates compliance with regulatory and industry standards**

Effective management of the equipment life cycle helps organizations meet regulatory requirements and adhere to industry standards. By maintaining equipment in accordance with relevant regulations and standards, businesses can avoid penalties, legal issues, and reputational damage while ensuring the safety and quality of their operations.

## **12. Supports warranty and service agreement requirements**

Many equipment purchases come with warranties and service agreements that require specific maintenance and care. Managing the equipment life cycle ensures compliance with these requirements, protecting the warranty coverage and ensuring access to necessary support and services from equipment manufacturers or service providers.

## **13. Assists in managing equipment-related risks and liabilities**

Equipment failure or malfunction can have various risks and liabilities associated with it. By implementing a comprehensive equipment life cycle management strategy, organizations can assess and manage these risks effectively, including financial, operational, safety, and environmental risks. This proactive approach helps mitigate potential losses and liabilities.

## **14. Enhances asset value and resale potential**

Well-maintained equipment retains its value and has higher resale potential. By managing the equipment life cycle, organizations can ensure that assets are kept in good condition, maximizing their value over time. This becomes especially important when considering equipment upgrades or replacements in the future.

## **15. Enables accurate budgeting and cost control for equipment maintenance**

Effective equipment life cycle management provides organizations with a better understanding of maintenance costs and requirements. By analyzing historical maintenance data and considering factors such as equipment age, usage, and condition, accurate budgeting for maintenance expenses can be achieved, leading to improved cost control and resource allocation.

## **16. Facilitates effective spare parts and inventory management**

Managing the equipment life cycle involves tracking and managing spare parts and inventory effectively. By maintaining accurate records of required spare parts, optimizing inventory levels, and implementing efficient procurement and storage processes, organizations can minimize equipment downtime and ensure timely availability of necessary components.

## **17. Supports effective decision-making regarding equipment repairs, replacements, and upgrades**

By analyzing equipment performance data and considering factors such as maintenance costs, reliability, and technological advancements, organizations can make informed decisions regarding equipment repairs, replacements, and upgrades. This helps optimize equipment utilization, minimize operational risks,  
and ensure that investments in equipment align with the overall business strategy and objectives.

## **18. Enables data-driven insights into equipment performance and maintenance trends**

Managing the equipment life cycle involves collecting and analyzing data related to equipment performance, maintenance activities, and downtime. By leveraging this data, organizations can gain valuable insights into equipment performance trends, identify patterns of failure, and make data-driven decisions to improve maintenance practices and optimize equipment reliability.

## **19. Enhances asset tracking and inventory management**

Effective equipment life cycle management includes robust asset tracking systems that provide real-time visibility into the location, condition, and usage of equipment. This enables organizations to optimize asset utilization, prevent loss or theft, and streamline inventory management processes for better operational efficiency.

## **20. Provides documentation and historical records for equipment maintenance and repairs**

Maintaining detailed documentation and historical records of equipment maintenance and repairs is essential for tracking equipment performance, compliance, and warranty claims. Proper documentation ensures transparency, facilitates audits, and supports troubleshooting and decision-making processes related to equipment maintenance.

## **21. Facilitates effective vendor and supplier management**

Managing the equipment life cycle involves establishing and maintaining relationships with vendors and suppliers. By effectively managing these relationships, organizations can ensure timely delivery of spare parts, access to technical expertise, and favorable pricing terms, thereby minimizing disruptions and optimizing equipment maintenance processes.

## **22. Supports equipment lifecycle planning and replacement strategies**

Effective equipment life cycle management includes developing long-term strategies for equipment lifecycle planning and replacements. By considering factors such as technological advancements, asset depreciation, and changing business needs, organizations can strategically plan equipment upgrades, replacements, or retirements to ensure the continuous availability of reliable and efficient equipment.

## **23. Assists in evaluating equipment reliability and performance metrics**

Managing the equipment life cycle involves monitoring and evaluating equipment reliability and performance metrics. By tracking key performance indicators (KPIs), organizations can assess equipment health, identify areas for improvement, and implement targeted measures to enhance reliability, reduce failures, and optimize equipment performance.

## **24. Improves the overall operational reliability and availability of equipment**

Effective equipment life cycle management aims to maximize operational reliability and availability. By implementing preventive maintenance strategies, conducting regular inspections, and addressing maintenance needs promptly, organizations can minimize equipment downtime, improve operational continuity, and ensure that equipment is available when needed.

## **25. Enables efficient utilization of equipment resources**

Managing the equipment life cycle ensures that equipment resources are utilized efficiently. By optimizing maintenance schedules, streamlining processes, and minimizing unplanned downtime, organizations can maximize the utilization of their equipment, reducing idle time and improving overall operational efficiency.

## **26. Supports continuous improvement initiatives for equipment maintenance processes**

Effective equipment life cycle management involves a commitment to continuous improvement. By analyzing maintenance data, seeking feedback from maintenance teams, and implementing process enhancements, organizations can continuously refine their equipment maintenance processes, identify opportunities for optimization, and drive ongoing improvements in efficiency and effectiveness.

## **27. Enhances communication and collaboration among equipment stakeholders**

Managing the equipment life cycle requires collaboration and communication among various stakeholders, including maintenance teams, operations personnel, and management. By promoting effective communication channels and fostering collaboration, organizations can align goals, share insights, and ensure that equipment maintenance efforts are coordinated, resulting in improved outcomes.

## **28. Helps in achieving and maintaining high-quality product standards**

Effective equipment life cycle management plays a crucial role in achieving and maintaining high-quality product standards. Well-maintained equipment contributes to consistent product quality, reduces the risk of defects or malfunctions, and ensures that products meet customer expectations and regulatory requirements. By prioritizing equipment maintenance, organizations can uphold their reputation for delivering high-quality products.

## **29. Assists in meeting customer expectations and delivery deadlines**

Timely and reliable equipment is essential for meeting customer expectations and delivery deadlines. By managing the equipment life cycle effectively, organizations can minimize equipment failures, maintain consistent production schedules, and fulfill customer orders on time. This enhances customer satisfaction, strengthens relationships, and improves overall business performance.

## **30. Promotes a culture of proactive equipment management and preventive maintenance**

Managing the equipment life cycle fosters a culture of proactive equipment management and preventive maintenance within an organization. By emphasizing the importance of regular inspections, maintenance planning, and proactive measures, employees become more aware of the critical role they play in equipment reliability and performance. This culture shift leads to increased accountability, better maintenance practices, and improved overall operational efficiency.

## **Conclusion**

Effective management of the equipment life cycle is vital for organizations to maximize ROI, optimize equipment performance, minimize downtime, and reduce risks. By implementing comprehensive asset management and plant maintenance strategies, businesses can extend the operational lifespan of equipment, enhance safety, and improve productivity. Additionally, proper equipment maintenance enables accurate budgeting, supports decision-making, and provides valuable data-driven insights. To ensure your organization benefits from these advantages, reach out to MaintWiz and unlock the full potential of managing your equipment life cycle.

Equipment Lifecycle
===================

Machinery, equipment and other physical assets are the foundation of industrial enterprises. Factories, plants and similar organizations depend on many pieces of equipment to support their day-to-day operations. Because of this heavy reliance on physical assets, unplanned downtime, outages and equipment failure can cause enormous setbacks for these organizations, resulting in decreased productivity and profits. It’s understandable that industrial enterprises strive to protect their assets to prevent equipment breakdowns and sunk costs.

Physical assets are a huge investment of time and money, and maximizing them for all they are worth involves proper equipment lifecycle management. Through strategic management of the equipment lifecycle, plants and similar organizations can optimize their assets for long-lasting performance and improved ROI.

* * *

What is the equipment lifecycle?
--------------------------------

“Equipment lifecycle” describes the overall lifecycle of a physical asset such as a piece of machinery or equipment. An asset can be as small as a power drill or as large as a jet plane. Regardless of size, every physical asset plays a critical role in supporting the overall productivity of an organization. Therefore, enterprises that heavily rely on physical assets for their operations understand just how important it is to optimize these assets for success. Part of this optimization process involves maximizing the equipment lifecycle so companies can reap the benefits of their assets for as long as possible.

The equipment lifecycle consists of four phases: planning, procurement/acquisition, operation/maintenance and disposal. Each equipment lifecycle phase is critical in supporting the longevity and performance of an asset.

*   **Planning -**The first equipment lifecycle stage is planning for replacing or acquiring a new piece of equipment. Proper planning involves assessing the organization’s needs and determining the most cost-effective strategy for procuring a new asset.

*   **Procurement/Acquisition -**The second phase of the equipment lifecycle is procurement, which means acquiring or purchasing the asset. Before purchase, companies must budget for the asset and negotiate costs to ensure the purchase is as cost-effective as possible. Once the purchase is finalized, the asset is assembled and added to the company’s inventory.

*   **Operation/Maintenance -**This next stage is, ideally, the longest stage of the equipment lifecycle. “Operation” means using the asset for its intended purpose, and “maintenance” means carefully maintaining an asset to support its performance over time. Strategic asset maintenance often incorporates tools such as [CMMS maintenance](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/cmms-maintenance)software and [predictive maintenance tools](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/predictive-maintenance-tools)to manage and optimize the maintenance process throughout the equipment lifecycle. Organizations can further maximize the operation/maintenance stage by using an [asset management solution](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/asset-management-solution)such as [EAM software](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/eam-software)to optimize equipment performance throughout the equipment lifecycle.

*   **Disposal -**The final stage of the equipment lifecycle involves properly disposing of the asset. This stage cannot be overlooked, as many organizations are required to follow certain environmental and safety regulations when discarding equipment. The equipment disposal phase entails safely dismantling and disposing of the asset while adhering to important regulations to protect employees and the surrounding environment from any hazards.

* * *

Equipment lifecycle optimization for enhanced results
-----------------------------------------------------

Thanks to modern technology, equipment lifecycle management has never been easier to harness. With tools such as [enterprise asset management](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/enterprise-asset-management)software ([EAM software](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/eam-software)), CMMS maintenance software and other intelligent solutions, organizations can take better control of the [enterprise asset management](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/enterprise-asset-management)process to maximize equipment performance over time. Here are just a few examples of asset management solutions that help support [asset lifecycle management](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/asset-lifecycle-management):

*   **[EAM software](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/eam-software) -**[EAM software](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/eam-software) supports the [asset lifecycle management](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/asset-lifecycle-management)process by overseeing all phases of the equipment lifecycle. This powerful software aims to optimize the utilization and quality of assets throughout their lifecycle to improve uptime and decrease operational costs.

*   **[CMMS software](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/cmms-software)-**[CMMS software](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/cmms-software) may be used as a standalone product or as a component of [EAM software](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/eam-software)to support the operation/maintenance stage of the equipment lifecycle. A CMMS centralizes important asset information to streamline equipment maintenance and improve overall efficiency.

*   **[Predictive maintenance tools](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/predictive-maintenance-tools)-**Tools such as sensors and gauges can be used in the operation/maintenance stage to detect warning signs of equipment malfunction. Companies can then analyze and apply this information to schedule preventive maintenance and reduce unplanned downtime.

* * *

Why equipment lifecycle management matters
------------------------------------------

For plants, factories and similar enterprises, the performance of their physical assets is directly correlated to profitability. The longer a physical asset can be used, the greater the return on investment. However, utilization only goes so far without optimization, which is where equipment lifecycle management comes into play.

Strategic [asset lifecycle management](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/asset-lifecycle-management)requires analyzing equipment performance and making adjustments accordingly to optimize assets for results. By taking advantage of tools such as [EAM software](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/eam-software)and other asset management solutions, companies can take a proactive, hands-on approach to asset management and gain a better understanding of how to effectively support the equipment lifecycle.

 The term “equipment lifecycle” describes the lifespan or longevity of a physical asset, including equipment and machinery. Equipment lifecycle is an important factor in productivity and throughput because the longer a piece of equipment can be used effectively, the better its return on investment. Therefore, companies who rely on physical assets often prioritize maximizing their equipment lifecycle through strategic [enterprise asset management](https://www.aspentech.com/en/cp/enterprise-asset-management).

**What are the four phases of the equipment lifecycle?**

 The four phases of the equipment lifecycle are planning, procurement/acquisition, operation/maintenance, and disposal.

*   **Planning**entails preparing for making an equipment purchase. This stage involves assessing the organization’s needs and determining the best course of action to suit these needs.

Companies must budget accordingly to ensure that the purchase is as cost-efficient as possible. # The Benefits of End-to-End Product Lifecycle Management for Manufacturers

![Picture of Tamizh Inian](https://frigate.ai/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/Tamizh-300x300.png)

##### Tamizh Inian

![product lifecycle management](https://frigate.ai/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/PLM-1.png)

#### Table of Contents

Are manufacturers struggling with tracking products’ journey from design to end-of-life? Managing the product lifecycle can often seem overwhelming with its many stages, departments, and ever-changing data. Without the right system, things can go wrong fast, leading to wasted resources, missed deadlines, and even product recalls. How can manufacturers ensure smooth, efficient product development while staying ahead in this competitive market?

The answer lies in **Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)**. In this blog, we will explore how **end-to-end PLM** can help manufacturers streamline operations, cut costs, and ensure product quality and compliance while improving collaboration across teams.

## What Is Product Lifecycle Management?

**Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)** is a comprehensive approach to managing a product’s entire lifecycle, from its initial concept to its final disposal. It involves integrating people, processes, business systems, and information to facilitate efficient and effective product management from start to finish.

An **end-to-end PLM** system encompasses all product lifecycle management stages—design, manufacturing, maintenance, and disposal. This ensures that data flows seamlessly between every department and stakeholder involved in the product’s creation and management. Instead of using isolated tools or disconnected systems, PLM brings everything together into a single unified platform.

A fully integrated **PLM system** allows manufacturers to track critical data—such as design specifications, material selection, process documentation, supplier performance, regulatory compliance, and customer feedback—all in one place.

## The Challenges Manufacturers Face in Product Lifecycle Management

Managing the product lifecycle presents numerous challenges for manufacturers. These pain points can negatively impact efficiency, product quality, and [time-to-market](https://frigate.ai/assembly/how-to-reduce-time-to-market-with-agile-manufacturing-and-value-engineering/). Below are some of the key hurdles manufacturers often encounter:

![PRODUCT LIFECYCLE MANAGEMENT](https://frigate.ai/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/image-56-1024x613.png "The Benefits of End-to-End Product Lifecycle Management for Manufacturers  1")

### Coordination Between Teams

Manufacturers typically involve multiple departments in product development, such as design, engineering, procurement, production, and quality control. Each department uses different tools and processes to complete its tasks, which can create communication barriers and lead to delays or mistakes. Without centralized access to product data, departments often work with outdated or inaccurate information, resulting in costly errors or misalignments.

**Example**: In a manual workflow, if the engineering team changes a design, the production team may not receive the updated version promptly. This could lead to the production of incorrect components, causing delays and additional costs.

### Inconsistent Data and Version Control

Maintaining accurate version control is crucial, especially when multiple stakeholders work on the same product design. Without a PLM system, manufacturers can struggle with managing various iterations of product designs, specifications, and updates. This can lead to confusion, errors, and wasted resources when team members inadvertently work with outdated data or conflicting design versions.

**Example**: A design team might modify a product without properly updating the rest of the stakeholders. As a result, the manufacturing team might work with the old design version, leading to parts that don’t fit or perform as expected.

### Lack of Integration with Other Systems

Many manufacturers rely on multiple business systems like ERP, CRM, and SCM software. Without proper integration, these systems often operate in silos, making it difficult to share data between them. This disconnect results in inefficiencies, such as duplicating efforts, delays in production, or mismatched inventory levels.

**Example**: If an ERP system is not integrated with the PLM system, updates to inventory levels might not be reflected in the product design, leading to material shortages and production delays.

### Poor Visibility Across the Product Lifecycle

Without an integrated system that provides real-time data, monitoring the [entire product lifecycle](https://frigate.ai/forging/how-forged-metal-parts-improves-the-product-service-life/) becomes challenging. Lack of visibility into design changes, production bottlenecks, or supplier performance can make identifying risks and making informed decisions difficult. This can delay the product’s launch and increase the likelihood of quality issues.

**Example**: A manufacturer might not realize a component has been delayed until it’s too late, affecting the overall production schedule and potentially leading to customer dissatisfaction.

## How End-to-End PLM Can Solve These Challenges

Manufacturers often face challenges such as design inefficiencies, production delays, and quality control issues. These obstacles can increase costs and slow down time to market. An **End-to-End Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)** system solves these problems by integrating every product lifecycle stage into one platform. From design to production, PLM streamlines processes [improves collaboration](https://frigate.ai/supplychain/the-importance-of-supplier-collaboration-in-achieving-manufacturing-excellence/) and ensures better quality control.

![PRODUCT LIFECYCLE MANAGEMENT](https://frigate.ai/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/image-58.png "The Benefits of End-to-End Product Lifecycle Management for Manufacturers  2")

Let’s explore how an integrated PLM solution can address these challenges and improve efficiency.

### Centralized Data for Better Collaboration

End-to-end PLM systems centralize all product data into one accessible platform, making it available to all departments in real-time. This means that as soon as a change is made in the design or material selection, all relevant teams (e.g., production, procurement, and quality control) are automatically notified. This level of collaboration helps avoid miscommunication and ensures that everyone uses the latest data.

**Example**: In a manufacturing company using an end-to-end PLM system, if a design change is made, the updated specifications are immediately accessible to the production team, who can proceed without waiting for email approvals or manual file transfers.

### Automated Workflows and Task Management

PLM systems help streamline processes by automating workflows, approvals, and task assignments. When a task is completed (e.g., a design update, approval process, or supplier selection), the system can automatically trigger the next steps, reducing delays and ensuring no skipped steps. This can significantly improve efficiency and reduce bottlenecks in the product development process.

**Example**: After the engineering team submits the final design, an automated workflow can send it to the procurement team to start sourcing materials while also triggering a quality control check to ensure compliance with standards.

### Real-Time Collaboration

**End-to-end PLM** enables teams to collaborate in real-time, regardless of location. Cloud-based systems allow teams to work together across different time zones, reducing feedback delays and enabling quicker decision-making. This helps speed up the product development cycle, ensuring that products are delivered faster and with higher precision.

**Example**: Engineers in one country can submit changes to a design, which a team of designers in another country can instantly review. This faster feedback loop reduces the overall time spent on product iterations.

### Seamless Integration with Other Business Systems

One key feature of an effective **end-to-end PLM** system is its ability to integrate with other enterprise systems like ERP, CRM, and SCM. This integration ensures that data flows seamlessly between all parts of the business, preventing duplication and enhancing efficiency. Manufacturers can improve inventory management, streamline order processing, and align supply chains with production schedules by sharing real-time data across systems.

**Example**: A PLM system integrated with ERP software can automatically update inventory levels as materials are ordered and received. This helps avoid material shortages and ensures that production continues smoothly.

## Economic Benefits of Implementing End-to-End PLM

End-to-end Product Lifecycle Management (PLM) integrates all stages of a product’s lifecycle to help manufacturers optimize operations, reduce costs, and improve profitability. PLM enables cost savings, enhances efficiency, speeds up time to market, and improves product quality. Identifying inefficiencies early and automating processes drives better financial performance across the entire product lifecycle.

### Cost Savings Through Better Resource Management

Centralizing all product data and automating manufacturers can eliminate unnecessary steps, reduce waste, and optimize material usage. This not only saves costs but also improves overall operational efficiency. For example, designers can use PLM to evaluate whether certain materials can be replaced with less expensive or more sustainable alternatives without compromising quality.

**Statistic**: Companies that adopt PLM solutions report a 20-30% reduction in production costs due to more efficient resource management and fewer design errors.

### Faster Time-to-Market

End-to-end PLM helps reduce the time it takes to develop products by eliminating delays, improving team collaboration, and automating key processes. Faster time-to-market allows manufacturers to capitalize on new opportunities and respond to market demand more quickly, which is critical in a competitive environment.

**Example**: By integrating design, procurement, and manufacturing data into one platform, manufacturers can speed up product design iterations and ensure faster approval processes, ultimately reducing time-to-market.

**Statistic**: [According to Deloitte](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deloitte), organizations that use PLM experience up to a 30% reduction in time-to-market.

### Improved Product Quality

End-to-end PLM ensures that every part of the product development process meets established quality standards. Every team has access to consistent, up-to-date information from design to production, reducing the risk of errors and quality issues. Additionally, manufacturers can track performance metrics and customer feedback to continuously improve product quality.

**Statistic**: PLM systems can reduce product defects by up to 50% by allowing manufacturers to identify potential issues earlier in development.

## Ensuring Compliance and Quality Control with PLM

Ensuring compliance and quality control is crucial in manufacturing. **Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)** helps meet these needs by integrating all product stages into one system. PLM tracks and manages compliance from design to production, ensuring standards are met. This reduces risks, minimizes recalls, and builds customer trust throughout the lifecycle.

![PRODUCT LIFECYCLE MANAGEMENT](https://frigate.ai/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/image-59.png "The Benefits of End-to-End Product Lifecycle Management for Manufacturers  3")

### **Regulation and Standards Adherence**

Many industries, such as [automotive](https://frigate.ai/industry/automotive/) and [aerospace](https://frigate.ai/industry/aerospace/), require strict adherence to regulatory standards and certifications. PLM systems help manufacturers ensure compliance by keeping track of all required documentation, certifications, and audits. By automating compliance checks and regulatory reporting, manufacturers can avoid non-compliance risks, such as fines or recalls.

**Example**: PLM systems in the aerospace industry ensure that all parts are manufactured according to FAA regulations, and the necessary certificates and test reports are stored for easy access during inspections.

### Traceability and Auditing

End-to-end PLM allows manufacturers to track every aspect of the product’s journey, from raw materials to final assembly. This traceability is essential for auditing purposes and helps manufacturers respond quickly to quality issues or product recalls.

**Example**: If a product defect is discovered in the field, a manufacturer can use PLM to trace the component’s production process, identify the batch of materials used, and determine where the issue occurred in the product lifecycle.

## Frigate’s Capabilities in End-to-End Product Lifecycle Management

Frigate Die Casting Services offers a comprehensive, integrated **Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)** solution designed to optimize the entire product development process for manufacturers. The company leverages its deep expertise in **die casting** to support each stage of the product lifecycle—from initial design through production, quality assurance, and regulatory compliance.

Frigate’s PLM capabilities begin with **design optimization**, ensuring that products are engineered for manufacturability, cost-efficiency, and performance. Through advanced simulation and modeling tools, Frigate helps manufacturers refine designs to reduce material waste, improve part strength, and optimize manufacturability before production begins. This reduces costly revisions and accelerates time-to-market.

Frigate’s PLM solutions enable seamless integration with suppliers in [supply chain management,](https://frigate.ai/supplychain/fully-integrated-supply-chains-with-contract-manufacturers-for-greater-business-agility/) ensuring that materials and components are sourced efficiently. This integration helps avoid material shortages, reduces lead times, and ensures that all parts meet strict quality standards.

Frigate uses real-time data tracking to monitor manufacturing processes as products move into production, ensuring high-quality standards and minimizing defects. Moreover, Frigate’s PLM system ensures all necessary **compliance documentation** is generated and tracked, helping manufacturers meet regulatory requirements.

By integrating **manufacturing data** into one unified platform, Frigate empowers manufacturers to make informed, data-driven decisions that reduce costs, enhance product quality, and improve operational efficiency across the product lifecycle.

## Conclusion

Product lifecycle management (PLM) is no longer a luxury for manufacturers but a necessity. By implementing an integrated end-to-end PLM system, manufacturers can address many challenges— from improving collaboration and reducing costs to ensuring compliance and enhancing product quality. The ability to manage data, streamline workflows, and optimize processes provides manufacturers with the tools they need to stay competitive in today’s fast-paced market.

In pursuing innovation, businesses often find themselves trapped in a paradox. They invest in cutting-edge equipment, only to see it become outdated. This paradox raises a crucial question. Can you innovate without mastering the art of equipment lifecycle management?

Despite its importance, equipment lifecycle management remains uncharted territory for many businesses. Neglecting this aspect of operations can be devastating. Think: Soaring costs and an inability to adapt to evolving market demands.

Today, we confront the innovation paradox head-on. Discover how to break free from endless spending. Harness your equipment's true potential. Rework lifecycle management and redirect resources for innovation. This guide can show you how.

**Main takeaways from this article:**

*   Properly managing assets from acquisition to disposal reduces unexpected equipment breakdowns, lowers maintenance costs, and extends equipment lifespan.
*   Regular maintenance and timely upgrades improve equipment performance, ensuring reliable and efficient operations with minimal downtime.
*   Using data and analytics helps businesses make informed decisions about equipment upgrades, replacements, and budgeting, maximizing return on investment.
*   RedBeam’s asset management software provides real-time equipment tracking, real-time monitoring, centralized documentation, and data to help you optimize equipment performance and efficiency.

**What Is the Equipment Lifecycle?**
------------------------------------

The equipment management life cycle encompasses the entire journey of a piece of equipment, from initial acquisition to its eventual retirement. It includes stages such as proper planning, design, procurement, deployment, operation, maintenance, and disposal.

Each stage involves specific processes to ensure optimal performance, safety, and compliance with environmental regulations. Proper and careful planning at each phase maximizes efficiency, extends the equipment's lifespan, and reduces costs.

**Why Equipment Lifecycle Management Matters**
----------------------------------------------

Many businesses find it difficult to save for the future while investing in the present. Choosing cheaper, temporary fixes may save money initially, but it can lead to more expenses later on. These can include higher upkeep costs, frequent replacements, and operational problems. Investing in better equipment is more expensive but can [save money in the long run](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/c/capital-investment.asp#:~:text=By%20investing%20in%20new%20equipment,to%20cost%20savings%20over%20time.). These savings manifest through reduced equipment maintenance expenses, increased productivity, and minimized downtime.

**Equipment Lifecycle Example**
-------------------------------

Here’s an example of how the equipment lifecycle can progress. Let’s take a look at a single item. In this case, a laptop.

*   **Planning and design:** The IT department assesses the need for new laptops, considering factors like performance requirements, budget constraints, and compatibility with existing systems.
*   **Procurement:** Based on the specifications, the IT team researches various laptop models and vendors. They obtain competitive bids, negotiate terms, and select the most suitable laptops.
*   **Deployment:** Once the laptops arrive, the IT team prepares them for use, making sure the appropriate software and security measures are installed.
*   **Operation:** Employees receive their laptops, get any necessary training, and begin using them for daily tasks.
*   **Maintenance:** Throughout the laptop's lifespan, software updates and security patches are applied and performance is monitored to keep an eye on equipment health.
*   **Disposal:** When equipment becomes outdated or performance degrades beyond repair, laptops are securely wiped and disposed of. Any salvageable components are repurposed if possible.

This lifecycle ensures that the company maximizes the value of its IT assets while maintaining security and efficiency throughout its usable life.

**Benefits of Equipment Lifecycle Management**
----------------------------------------------

Benefits of long-term investments in equipment lifecycle management include:

### **Reduced Costs and Downtime**

Effective equipment life cycle management minimizes unexpected breakdowns and costly emergency repairs. By implementing proactive maintenance schedules and doing timely repairs and upgrades, businesses can reduce operational interruptions and extend the lifespan of their equipment.

This approach lowers repair expenses and minimizes unplanned downtime, ensuring consistent productivity and reducing revenue loss.

### **Improved Equipment Performance**

Well-managed equipment performs more efficiently and reliably. Regular maintenance tasks and timely upgrades prevent wear and tear, enhancing operational efficiency and safety. By monitoring performance metrics and addressing potential issues early, you can optimize your equipment's output and maintain high-quality standards, ultimately boosting productivity.

### **Stronger Return on Investment (ROI)**

Investing in proper equipment lifecycle management maximizes the value derived from each piece of equipment. By extending its operational life and maintaining optimal performance, businesses achieve a [stronger ROI](https://redbeam.com/roi). This approach reduces the need for frequent replacements and allows organizations to fully capitalize on their initial investments.

### **Improved Planning and Forecasting**

A comprehensive view of the equipment lifecycle enables better planning and budgeting. By understanding when you need equipment maintenance, upgrades, or replacement, businesses can make informed financial decisions. This foresight supports more accurate forecasting, ensuring resource availability and minimizing unexpected expenses.

### **Enhanced Compliance and Safety**

Effective lifecycle management helps businesses comply with industry standards and safety regulations. By maintaining equipment according to regulatory requirements, organizations minimize safety risks and avoid costly fines or legal complications. Ensuring safety also protects employees and promotes a healthier work environment.

### **Sustainability and Environmental Responsibility**

Properly managing equipment throughout its lifecycle supports sustainability efforts by minimizing waste and maximizing resource use. Proper disposal and recycling of outdated equipment reduce environmental impact. Additionally, energy-efficient upgrades can decrease overall energy consumption, contributing to a more sustainable operation.

**Key Stages of the Equipment Lifecycle**
-----------------------------------------

Effective equipment lifecycle management involves a series of stages, each playing a critical role in maximizing efficiency, reducing equipment costs, and ensuring the safe and sustainable use of equipment. Understanding these stages helps organizations make informed decisions, optimize operations, and strategically plan for future needs.

Let's explore each phase in detail:

### **1. Planning and Design**

Key steps:

*   Research and conceptualization of the equipment's specifications and requirements
*   Detailed planning involving engineers, designers, and stakeholders

During the planning and design phase, organizations identify their operational needs and outline the specifications required for new equipment. This stage involves extensive research, feasibility studies, and collaboration with engineers, designers, and stakeholders to ensure the equipment meets safety, efficiency, and regulatory standards.

By anticipating potential challenges and designing solutions accordingly, businesses can prevent costly redesigns or modifications later in the asset’s lifecycle.

### **2. Procurement and Acquisition**

Key steps:

*   Sourcing and purchasing the equipment from reliable suppliers
*   Negotiating contracts, ensuring quality standards, and budget adherence

Equipment purchases focus on selecting and acquiring the right equipment to meet organizational needs. It includes evaluating suppliers, negotiating contracts, and ensuring the equipment meets quality and budget requirements. As items are procured, they are entered into the asset management software systems and [asset tags](https://redbeam.com/features/equipment-tagging-software) are applied.

During this phase, you will also consider what data you want to include in your asset tags and asset tracking system. For example, for [IT hardware asset management](https://redbeam.com/features/it-asset-management-software), you may want to include equipment maintenance logging and condition assessment.

Proper procurement practices prevent delays, reduce costs, and ensure compliance with industry standards. Effective supplier relationships also pave the way for long-term support and proper equipment maintenance.

### **3. Installation and Commissioning**

Key steps:

*   Physical installation of the equipment at the designated location
*   Testing and calibrating to ensure proper functionality and safety

Once acquired, the equipment is installed at the designated site. This process involves setting up the equipment, integrating it with existing systems, and performing tests to verify its functionality, safety, and efficiency.

Proper commissioning ensures that the equipment operates as intended and meets all regulatory requirements. Any issues detected during this phase can be resolved before full-scale operation, minimizing potential disruptions.

### **4. Operation and Maintenance**

Key steps:

*   Regular operation of the equipment in alignment with manufacturer guidelines
*   Routine preventative maintenance to prevent breakdowns and ensure longevity

During this stage, the equipment is actively used in daily business operations. Routine maintenance of your tracked equipment, including inspections, cleaning, lubrication, and part replacements, is conducted to prevent malfunctions and extend the equipment’s lifespan.

Proper operation and proper maintenance practices enhance safety, efficiency, and productivity while minimizing unexpected downtime and costly repairs.

### **5. Upgrades and Modifications**

Key steps:

*   Implementing upgrades to enhance efficiency or adhere to new standards
*   Modifications to adapt the equipment to changing needs or technological advancements

To keep up with evolving technological standards and operational requirements, equipment may require upgrades or modifications. This phase involves enhancing existing systems to improve performance, safety, and efficiency.

Upgrades can include software updates, hardware enhancements, or adapting equipment to new regulations. Proactive upgrades help organizations maintain a competitive edge and extend the equipment's lifecycle.

### **6. Decommissioning and Disposal**

Key steps:

*   Shutting down the equipment at the end of its useful life
*   Disassembling, recycling, or disposing of components according to regulations

When equipment reaches the end of its useful life, it is decommissioned and safely removed from service. This stage includes shutting down operations, disassembling the equipment, and disposing of your valuable equipment or recycling components according to environmental regulations.

Proper decommissioning minimizes the environmental impact, ensures safety, and complies with legal standards.

### **7. Replacement**

Key steps:

*   Identifying suitable replacements for obsolete or inefficient equipment
*   Transitioning to new equipment to avoid disruptions

Replacement involves evaluating outdated or inefficient equipment and selecting suitable alternatives. This stage ensures a seamless transition to new equipment with minimal operational disruption.

Strategic replacement planning helps organizations maintain productivity while leveraging the latest technological advancements for improved efficiency and cost savings.

### **8. Asset Retirement**

Key steps:

*   Documenting the retirement process, including disposal methods and environmental impact
*   Archiving relevant data for future reference or compliance purposes

Asset retirement involves formally documenting the disposal of the retired equipment and accounting for any residual value in your accounting process. This step also includes archiving maintenance records, decommissioning procedures, and compliance reports.

Proper documentation ensures regulatory compliance and provides valuable insights for future procurement and lifecycle management decisions.

### **9. Monitoring and Optimization**

Key steps:

*   Continuous monitoring and continuous improvement of equipment performance and energy usage
*   Implementing optimizations based on data analysis to improve operations and cost-effectiveness.

Throughout the equipment lifecycle, continuous monitoring and data analysis of critical operations are key to maintaining optimal performance. By tracking key performance indicators (KPIs), energy consumption, and maintenance needs, organizations can proactively identify inefficiencies and implement cost-effective optimizations.

This stage supports data-driven decision-making and enhances the overall efficiency and sustainability of equipment operations.
